questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rope of diameter $1 \text{ cm}$ breaks when the tension applied exceeds $500 \text{ N}$. Determine the maximum tension that a similar rope with a diameter of $2 \text{ cm}$ can withstand.
questions.solution:
Breaking stress is a property of the material and remains constant for similar ropes.

Breaking stress is given by the ratio of breaking tension to the cross-sectional area:

$S = \dfrac{T}{A} = \dfrac{4T}{\pi d^2}$

Since $S$ is constant, the maximum tension $T$ is directly proportional to the square of the diameter $d$:

$T \propto d^2$

Using the given values, we can write:

$\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^2$

Substituting $T_1 = 500 \text{ N}$, $d_1 = 1 \text{ cm}$, and $d_2 = 2 \text{ cm}$:

$\dfrac{T_2}{500} = \left(\dfrac{2}{1}\right)^2$

$\dfrac{T_2}{500} = 4$

$T_2 = 2000 \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider two wires, $A$ and $B$, composed of the same material. Wire $A$ possesses a length $l$ and diameter $r$, whereas wire $B$ has a length $2l$ and diameter $r/2$. When both wires are stretched by an identical force, the ratio of the elongation in $A$ to the elongation in $B$ is
questions.solution:
Young's modulus is given by $Y = \dfrac{F L}{A \Delta L}$

The elongation of a wire is $\Delta L = \dfrac{F L}{A Y} = \dfrac{4 F L}{\pi d^2 Y}$

For wire $A$, length $L_A = l$ and diameter $d_A = r$. The elongation is:

$\Delta L_A = \dfrac{4 F l}{\pi r^2 Y}$

For wire $B$, length $L_B = 2l$ and diameter $d_B = r/2$. The elongation is:

$\Delta L_B = \dfrac{4 F (2l)}{\pi (r/2)^2 Y} = \dfrac{32 F l}{\pi r^2 Y}$

Taking the ratio of the elongations:

$\dfrac{\Delta L_A}{\Delta L_B} = \dfrac{\dfrac{4 F l}{\pi r^2 Y}}{\dfrac{32 F l}{\pi r^2 Y}} = \dfrac{4}{32} = \dfrac{1}{8}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a load $W$ is suspended from a wire, it elongates by $1.0 \text{ mm}$. If this same wire is passed over a pulley and a weight $W$ is attached to each of its two ends, the total elongation of the wire will be
questions.solution:
Let the original length of the wire be $L$, area of cross-section be $A$, and Young's modulus be $Y$.

When a load $W$ is suspended from the wire, the tension in the wire is $T = W$. The elongation is given by:

$\Delta L = \dfrac{WL}{AY} = 1.0 \text{ mm}$

When the same wire is passed over a pulley and a weight $W$ is attached to each of its two ends, the system is in equilibrium and the tension in the entire wire remains $T' = W$.

The total elongation of the wire is:

$\Delta L' = \dfrac{T'L}{AY} = \dfrac{WL}{AY} = 1.0 \text{ mm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The breaking stress of a wire is dependent on
options: the shape of the cross section
questions.solution:
Breaking stress is defined as the maximum force per unit area that a material can withstand before it breaks.

It is an intrinsic property of the material and is independent of the physical dimensions of the wire, such as its length, radius, or the shape of its cross-section.

Therefore, the breaking stress of a wire depends only on the material of the wire.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Before breaking, a wire is able to sustain a weight of $20 \text{ kg}$. If the wire is cut into two equal parts, each part can sustain a weight of
questions.solution:
The breaking force of a wire is given by $F = \text{Breaking stress} \times A$, where $A$ is the area of cross-section.

Breaking stress is a property of the material and is independent of the length of the wire.

When the wire is cut into two equal parts, the area of cross-section $A$ remains unchanged.

Therefore, the breaking force remains the same, and each part can sustain the same weight of $20 \text{ kg}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A heavy uniform rod hangs vertically from a fixed support and undergoes stretching due to its own weight. The diameter of this rod is
options: smallest at the top and progressively increases downwards
largest at the top and progressively decreases downwards
questions.solution:
Let the length of the rod be $L$, its total weight be $W$, and its initial cross-sectional area be $A$.

The tension $T$ at a distance $x$ from the bottom of the hanging rod is equal to the weight of the rod below that cross-section, given by $T = \dfrac{W x}{L}$.

The longitudinal stress at this point is $\sigma = \dfrac{T}{A} = \dfrac{W x}{A L}$.

The stress is maximum at the top support ($x = L$) and zero at the free bottom end ($x = 0$).

Longitudinal strain is directly proportional to longitudinal stress. According to Poisson's ratio, a longitudinal extension is accompanied by a lateral contraction, which is proportional to the longitudinal strain.

Since the longitudinal strain is maximum at the top, the lateral contraction is also maximum at the top.

Therefore, the diameter of the rod is smallest at the top and progressively increases downwards.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As a metal wire is stretched by a load, the fractional change in its volume $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}$ is proportional to
questions.solution:
Volume of a cylindrical wire is $V = \pi r^2 l$

Differentiating to find the fractional change in volume:

$\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = 2\dfrac{\Delta r}{r} + \dfrac{\Delta l}{l}$

Poisson's ratio $\sigma$ is defined as $\sigma = -\dfrac{\Delta r / r}{\Delta l / l}$

$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta r}{r} = -\sigma \dfrac{\Delta l}{l}$

Substituting this into the expression for fractional change in volume:

$\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = -2\sigma \dfrac{\Delta l}{l} + \dfrac{\Delta l}{l} = (1 - 2\sigma)\dfrac{\Delta l}{l}$

Since $\sigma$ is a constant for a given material, $(1 - 2\sigma)$ is constant.

Therefore, $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} \propto \dfrac{\Delta l}{l}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metal wire has a length of $l_1$ when subjected to a tension $T_1$, and a length of $l_2$ when the tension is $T_2$. The natural length of the wire is
questions.solution:
Let the natural length of the wire be $L$, cross-sectional area be $A$, and Young's modulus be $Y$.

The extension in the wire under tension $T_1$ is $\Delta l_1 = l_1 - L$.

From Hooke's law, $Y = \dfrac{T_1 L}{A \Delta l_1} \Rightarrow T_1 = \dfrac{YA}{L}(l_1 - L)$.

Similarly, the extension under tension $T_2$ is $\Delta l_2 = l_2 - L$.

$T_2 = \dfrac{YA}{L}(l_2 - L)$.

Dividing the two equations, we get:

$\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = \dfrac{l_1 - L}{l_2 - L}$

$T_1 l_2 - T_1 L = T_2 l_1 - T_2 L$

$L(T_2 - T_1) = l_1 T_2 - l_2 T_1$

$L = \dfrac{l_1 T_2 - l_2 T_1}{T_2 - T_1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A heavy mass is fastened to a thin wire and whirled in a vertical circle. The wire is most likely to break
options: when the mass reaches the highest point
at an angle of $\cos^{-1}(1/3)$ from the upward vertical
when the mass reaches the lowest point
questions.solution:
Let $m$ be the mass, $v$ be the velocity, and $r$ be the radius of the vertical circle.

At an angle $\theta$ from the lowest point, the tension $T$ in the wire is given by the equation:

$T - mg \cos \theta = \dfrac{mv^2}{r}$

$T = \dfrac{mv^2}{r} + mg \cos \theta$

By conservation of mechanical energy, the velocity $v$ of the mass is maximum at the lowest point of the vertical circle. Also, the value of $\cos \theta$ is maximum at the lowest point since $\theta = 0$ and $\cos 0 = 1$.

Therefore, both terms $\dfrac{mv^2}{r}$ and $mg \cos \theta$ are maximum at the lowest point, making the tension $T$ maximum at this position.

Since the wire is most likely to break when the tension is maximum, it will break when the mass reaches the lowest point.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As a metal wire elongates due to a load suspended from it, the gravitational potential energy decreases.
options: This energy is shared between the increased elastic potential energy of the wire and heat.
This energy is entirely converted into the increased elastic potential energy of the wire.
This energy is entirely converted into the increased kinetic energy of the block.
This energy is entirely dissipated as heat.
questions.solution:
When a load of mass $M$ is suspended from a metal wire, the wire elongates by a distance $x$ under the constant force $F = Mg$.

The decrease in the gravitational potential energy of the load is $\Delta U = Mgx$.

The elastic potential energy stored in the stretched wire is $U_{elastic} = \dfrac{1}{2} F x = \dfrac{1}{2} Mgx$.

The remaining energy is $\Delta U - U_{elastic} = Mgx - \dfrac{1}{2}Mgx = \dfrac{1}{2}Mgx$.

This remaining energy is dissipated as heat. Thus, the decrease in gravitational potential energy is shared between the increased elastic potential energy of the wire and heat.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When we speak of the surface of a liquid, we are referring to
options: a layer with a thickness on the order of $10^{-8} \text{ m}$
all the molecules exposed to the atmosphere
a layer with a thickness on the order of $10^{-4} \text{ m}$
questions.solution:
The surface of a liquid is not a mere geometrical plane but a layer of finite thickness known as the surface film.

The thickness of this layer is equal to the molecular range, which is the maximum distance up to which the intermolecular forces of attraction are effective.

The molecular range for liquids is of the order of $10^{-9} \text{ m}$ to $10^{-8} \text{ m}$.

Therefore, the surface of a liquid refers to a layer with a thickness on the order of $10^{-8} \text{ m}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Within a gravity-free hall, an ice cube is suspended in a vacuum. As the ice melts, it
options: will change its shape to a sphere
will fall down onto the floor of the hall
questions.solution:
In a gravity-free environment, the effective acceleration due to gravity is zero. Thus, the ice cube will neither fall down nor fly upwards.

As the ice melts into water, the intermolecular forces, specifically surface tension, become the dominant force acting on the liquid.

To minimize its surface energy, the liquid tends to acquire the shape with the minimum surface area for a given volume, which is a sphere.

Therefore, the melting ice will change its shape to a sphere.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As water droplets combine to form a larger drop,
options: energy may be either liberated or absorbed depending on the nature of the liquid
energy is neither liberated nor absorbed
questions.solution:
When $n$ small droplets of radius $r$ combine to form a single large drop of radius $R$, the total volume remains constant.

$\dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3 = n \times \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \Rightarrow R = n^{1/3}r$

The initial total surface area is $A_i = n \times 4\pi r^2$.

The final surface area is $A_f = 4\pi R^2 = 4\pi n^{2/3}r^2$.

Since $n > n^{2/3}$ for $n > 1$, the total surface area decreases ($A_f < A_i$).

Surface energy is directly proportional to the surface area ($U = T \times A$, where $T$ is surface tension). As the surface area decreases, the total surface energy of the system decreases.

This loss in surface energy is liberated to the surroundings.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The dimensional formula $ML^{-1}T^{-2}$ can represent
questions.solution:
The dimensional formula for coefficient of viscosity is $[\eta] = \dfrac{[F]}{[A][\dfrac{dv}{dx}]} = \dfrac{MLT^{-2}}{L^2 T^{-1}} = ML^{-1}T^{-1}$.

The dimensional formula for surface tension is $[S] = \dfrac{[F]}{[L]} = \dfrac{MLT^{-2}}{L} = MT^{-2}$.

The dimensional formula for modulus of elasticity is $[E] = \dfrac{\text{Stress}}{\text{Strain}} = \dfrac{[F]/[A]}{1} = \dfrac{MLT^{-2}}{L^2} = ML^{-1}T^{-2}$.

The dimensional formula for moment of a force is $[\tau] = [r][F] = L \cdot MLT^{-2} = ML^2T^{-2}$.

Thus, $ML^{-1}T^{-2}$ represents modulus of elasticity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Air is pumped into a soap bubble of radius $r$, doubling its radius. Given that the surface tension of the soap solution is $S$, the work done during this process is
questions.solution:
A soap bubble has two free surfaces, an inner surface and an outer surface.

Initial surface area of the soap bubble, $A_1 = 2 \times 4\pi r^2 = 8\pi r^2$

Final radius of the soap bubble is $2r$.

Final surface area of the soap bubble, $A_2 = 2 \times 4\pi (2r)^2 = 32\pi r^2$

Change in surface area, $\Delta A = A_2 - A_1 = 32\pi r^2 - 8\pi r^2 = 24\pi r^2$

Work done in increasing the radius is equal to the change in surface energy.

$W = S \times \Delta A = S \times 24\pi r^2 = 24\pi r^2 S$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When additional air is forced into a soap bubble, the pressure inside it
questions.solution:
The pressure inside a soap bubble of radius $R$ and surface tension $T$ is given by $P = P_0 + \dfrac{4T}{R}$, where $P_0$ is the atmospheric pressure.

When additional air is forced into the soap bubble, its size increases, meaning its radius $R$ increases.

Since the excess pressure $\Delta P = \dfrac{4T}{R}$ is inversely proportional to the radius, an increase in $R$ leads to a decrease in the excess pressure.

Therefore, the total pressure inside the soap bubble decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If two soap bubbles of unequal radii are connected by a tube,
options: there is no flow of air
air flows from the smaller bubble to the larger bubble
air flows from the larger bubble to the smaller bubble until their sizes are interchanged
air flows from the larger bubble to the smaller bubble until their sizes become equal
questions.solution:
The excess pressure inside a soap bubble of radius $R$ is given by $P = \dfrac{4T}{R}$, where $T$ is the surface tension.

For a smaller bubble, the radius $R$ is less, so the excess pressure $P$ is greater.

When two bubbles of unequal radii are connected by a tube, air flows from the region of higher pressure to the region of lower pressure.

Therefore, air flows from the smaller bubble to the larger bubble. As air flows out of the smaller bubble, its radius decreases further, increasing the pressure even more, while the larger bubble grows.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capillary tube of radius $r$ is immersed in water, as shown in the figure. Given that the atmospheric pressure is $P_0$, what is the pressure at point $A$?
questions.solution:
The pressure on the concave side of a liquid meniscus is greater than the pressure on the convex side by an amount $\dfrac{2S}{R}$, where $S$ is the surface tension and $R$ is the radius of curvature of the meniscus.

For water in a capillary tube, the meniscus is concave upwards. The air is on the concave side and the liquid is on the convex side.

The pressure just above the meniscus is the atmospheric pressure $P_0$. Point A is located just below the meniscus, on the convex side.

Assuming the angle of contact for water and glass to be zero, the radius of curvature $R$ is equal to the radius of the capillary tube $r$.

Therefore, the pressure difference across the meniscus is given by:

$P_{\text{above}} - P_A = \dfrac{2S}{r}$

Substituting $P_{\text{above}} = P_0$, we get:

$P_0 - P_A = \dfrac{2S}{r}$

$P_A = P_0 - \dfrac{2S}{r}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The excess pressure within a soap bubble is twice the excess pressure within a second soap bubble. If the volume of the first bubble is $n$ times the volume of the second bubble, the value of $n$ is
questions.solution:
The excess pressure inside a soap bubble of radius $R$ and surface tension $T$ is given by $P = \dfrac{4T}{R}$.

Given that $P_1 = 2P_2$, we can write:

$\dfrac{4T}{R_1} = 2 \left( \dfrac{4T}{R_2} \right)$

$R_2 = 2R_1 \Rightarrow \dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{1}{2}$

The volume of a soap bubble is $V = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3$.

Given that $V_1 = nV_2$, we have:

$n = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \left( \dfrac{R_1}{R_2} \right)^3$

Substituting the ratio of radii:

$n = \left( \dfrac{1}{2} \right)^3 = \dfrac{1}{8} = 0.125$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the graphs below could represent the relationship between the capillary rise $h$ and the radius $r$ of the capillary?
questions.solution:
The capillary rise $h$ in a tube of radius $r$ is given by Jurin's law:

$h = \dfrac{2T \cos \theta}{\rho g r}$

where $T$ is the surface tension, $\theta$ is the contact angle, $\rho$ is the density of the liquid, and $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity.

For a given liquid and capillary tube material, the terms $T$, $\theta$, $\rho$, and $g$ are constant. Thus, the relationship between $h$ and $r$ is:

$h \propto \dfrac{1}{r}$

This indicates that $h$ is inversely proportional to $r$, and the equation $hr = \text{constant}$ represents a rectangular hyperbola.

Looking at the given graphs, curve 'c' is a rectangular hyperbola, which correctly represents this inverse relationship.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Water ascends to a length of $10 \text{ cm}$ in a vertical capillary tube. If the tube is inclined at $45^\circ$, the length of water risen in the tube will be
questions.solution:
The vertical height of water in the capillary tube is $h = 10 \text{ cm}$.

When the capillary tube is inclined at an angle $\alpha$ with the vertical, the vertical height of the liquid column remains constant.

Let $l$ be the length of the liquid column in the inclined tube.

From the geometry of the setup, $h = l \cos \alpha$.

Given $\alpha = 45^\circ$ and $h = 10 \text{ cm}$.

$10 = l \cos 45^\circ$

$10 = l \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$

$l = 10\sqrt{2} \text{ cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capillary tube $20 \text{ cm}$ long is dipped in water, causing the water to rise up to $8 \text{ cm}$. If the complete arrangement is placed inside a freely falling elevator, determine the length of the water column in the capillary tube.
questions.solution:
The height of capillary rise is given by the formula:

$h = \dfrac{2T \cos \theta}{\rho r g_{\text{eff}}}$

In a freely falling elevator, the effective acceleration due to gravity is zero ($g_{\text{eff}} = 0$).

As $g_{\text{eff}} \rightarrow 0$, the theoretical height $h \rightarrow \infty$. However, the liquid cannot overflow from the upper end of the capillary tube. Instead, the liquid rises to the top of the tube and the meniscus adjusts its radius of curvature $R$ such that $h R = \text{constant}$.

Thus, the water will rise to the full length of the capillary tube.

Since the total length of the capillary tube is $20 \text{ cm}$, the length of the water column will be $20 \text{ cm}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Viscosity is a characteristic of
questions.solution:
Viscosity is the property of a fluid by virtue of which an internal frictional force comes into play when the fluid is in motion, opposing the relative motion between its different layers.

Since both liquids and gases are classified as fluids and have the ability to flow, viscosity is a characteristic property of both liquids and gases.

Solids do not flow, so they do not exhibit viscosity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Fundamentally, the force of viscosity is
questions.solution:
Viscosity is the internal friction of a fluid that arises due to intermolecular forces between the adjacent layers of the fluid.

Intermolecular forces, such as van der Waals forces, hydrogen bonding, and dipole-dipole interactions, are fundamentally caused by the electric and magnetic interactions between the charged particles (electrons and protons) within the atoms and molecules.

Therefore, the force of viscosity is fundamentally electromagnetic in nature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The viscous force present between two layers of a liquid is expressed as $\dfrac{F}{A} = -\eta \dfrac{dv}{dz}$. The quantity $F/A$ can be referred to as
questions.solution:
The viscous force $F$ acts parallel to the layers of the liquid to oppose their relative motion.

Since the force is applied tangentially to the surface area $A$ of the layers, the quantity $F/A$ represents the tangential force per unit area.

The tangential force per unit area is defined as tangential stress or shear stress.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A raindrop descending near the surface of the earth maintains an almost uniform velocity because
options: the surface tension force balances its weight
the viscous force of the air balances its weight
the atmospheric electric field balances the weight of the charged drops
questions.solution:
When a raindrop falls under gravity, it accelerates initially. As its velocity increases, the upward viscous drag force exerted by the air also increases.

According to Stokes' law, the viscous force is directly proportional to the velocity of the raindrop ($F_v = 6\pi\eta r v$).

When this upward viscous force becomes equal to the downward weight of the raindrop, the net force acting on it becomes zero.

As a result, the raindrop stops accelerating and continues to fall with a constant velocity, known as the terminal velocity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A piece of wood is submerged deep inside a long column of water and released. It will move upwards
questions.solution:
When the piece of wood is released, the forces acting on it are the upward buoyant force $F_B$, the downward weight $mg$, and the downward viscous drag force $F_v$.

The net upward force is given by $F_{net} = F_B - mg - F_v$.

Initially, the velocity is zero, so the viscous drag $F_v$ is zero. Since the density of wood is less than that of water, $F_B > mg$, and the wood accelerates upwards.

As the velocity of the wood increases, the viscous drag force $F_v$ also increases.

Consequently, the net upward force $F_{net}$ decreases, which means the upward acceleration $a = \dfrac{F_{net}}{m}$ decreases continuously.

Thus, the wood moves upwards with a decreasing upward acceleration.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a spherical ball is dropped into a long column of a viscous liquid, its speed as a function of time is best represented by the graph
questions.solution:
When a spherical ball is dropped into a long column of a viscous liquid, its initial velocity is zero.

The forces acting on the ball are its weight acting downwards, the buoyant force acting upwards, and the viscous drag force acting upwards. The net downward force is given by:
$F_{net} = W - F_B - F_v$

According to Stokes' law, the viscous drag force is proportional to the velocity of the ball ($F_v = 6\pi\eta r v$). As the velocity of the ball increases, the viscous drag force also increases, which causes the net downward force and the acceleration to decrease.

Eventually, the viscous drag force becomes large enough such that the net force becomes zero. At this point, the acceleration is zero, and the ball continues to move with a constant maximum velocity called the terminal velocity ($v_t$).

The velocity as a function of time is given by the relation:
$v(t) = v_t(1 - e^{-kt})$

This equation represents an exponentially increasing curve that starts from the origin and asymptotically approaches a constant value ($v_t$). Graph B correctly represents this behavior.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When falling in air, a solid sphere reaches a terminal velocity of $20 \text{ m/s}$. If it is instead allowed to fall in a vacuum,
options: the terminal velocity will be $20 \text{ m/s}$
the terminal velocity will be more than $20 \text{ m/s}$
there will be no terminal velocity
the terminal velocity will be less than $20 \text{ m/s}$
questions.solution:
Terminal velocity is the constant velocity attained by a falling body when the net force acting on it becomes zero. This happens when the downward gravitational force is balanced by the upward viscous drag and buoyant forces.

In a vacuum, there is no fluid to provide viscous drag or buoyant force. The only force acting on the sphere is its weight, which causes a constant downward acceleration equal to the acceleration due to gravity ($g$).

Since the sphere continuously accelerates under gravity, its velocity keeps increasing with time and it never attains a constant velocity. Therefore, there will be no terminal velocity.
